title: IVPN Review 2026: Gibraltar Privacy, Account-IDs Only, and the Honest Mullvad Alternative
metaDescription: Deep IVPN review for April 2026. Gibraltar jurisdiction explained (why it is not the 14 Eyes), account-number-only signup, seven Cure53 audits, Multi-hop, Hardcore Mode, port forwarding on Pro, and the honest case for paying more than Mullvad.
pros: Account-number-only signup — no email, no name, no password; your random account ID is your entire identity, the same anonymity model Mullvad pioneered
Gibraltar jurisdiction — British Overseas Territory with its own legal system, explicitly NOT a 14 Eyes signatory, and no mandatory VPN data retention law
Seven consecutive annual Cure53 audits (2019 through 2025) — one of the longest continuous independent-audit records in the entire VPN industry
Fully open-source clients on Windows, macOS, Linux, iOS, and Android at github.com/ivpn — code is verifiable, not just claimed
AntiTracker with Hardcore Mode — DNS-level blocker that optionally strips every Google and Facebook IP from your traffic for maximum anti-surveillance posture
Multi-hop on the Pro tier — route through two VPN servers in different jurisdictions, deliberately undoing any single-exit compromise
Port forwarding on the Pro tier — IVPN kept it when Mullvad removed it in July 2023, which makes IVPN the natural destination for privacy-first torrent seeders
Weekly billing option — $2 Standard / $4 Pro for seven days is genuine low-commitment access, functionally a paid trial, and a genuinely unusual pricing UX
Accepts cash in the post, Monero, Bitcoin, and Bitcoin Lightning — full anonymity chain from payment through account to exit IP
Warrant canary and published transparency reports — cryptographically signed, updated quarterly, documenting every government request received
WireGuard and OpenVPN only — no proprietary protocol wrappers that cannot be independently audited
Listed by PrivacyGuides.org as a recommended VPN in the tier-1 privacy category alongside Mullvad and Proton — a trust signal that matters in privacy-maximalist circles
cons: Small server network — roughly 100 servers across approximately 35 countries in 2026, a fraction of NordVPN (9,000+) or Proton (13,900+)
Standard plan caps at 2 simultaneous device connections, the lowest in the industry — even Mullvad allows 5 on a single plan
Pro plan caps at 7 devices — better than Standard, still below NordVPN's 10 and Surfshark's unlimited
Netflix, Disney+, BBC iPlayer, Prime Video, and Hulu are unreliably unblocked at best — IVPN does not market itself as a streaming VPN and does not maintain streaming infrastructure
More expensive than Mullvad on every time horizon — Mullvad is €5 flat, IVPN Standard is $6/month or $3.89/month on the 3-year plan
No live chat — email support only, with typical response times of hours to a business day rather than minutes
No browser extensions — Chrome, Firefox, and Edge users get no lightweight add-on; the position is that a full tunnel is the correct surface
No smart-TV or gaming-console apps — router configuration via WireGuard or OpenVPN is the only option for those devices
Small server network means fewer routing options for niche geographies — limited coverage across South America, Africa, the Middle East
Minimalist UI will feel under-polished to buyers coming from NordVPN or Surfshark's big-map, auto-connect aesthetics

testResults.downloadSpeed: 300-340 Mbps peak on nearest WireGuard server on a 330 Mbps baseline; 140-200 Mbps on transatlantic and long-distance routes
testResults.uploadSpeed: 240-280 Mbps on nearby servers, 100-170 Mbps on long-distance routes
testResults.latency: 12-18 ms on nearest server, 45-95 ms within Western Europe/US, 160-195 ms to Tokyo, 280-310 ms to Sydney
testResults.speedRetention: ~88-94% of baseline on nearby servers, 45-60% on long-distance — WireGuard retention is strong, but routing matters more than protocol on IVPN's small fleet
testResults.noLogsClaim: Seven consecutive annual Cure53 audits (2019, 2020, 2021, 2022, 2023, 2024 March, 2025 May). Scope has rotated across server infrastructure, apps, API, web UI, Email Forwarding service, and DNS service. Audit reports are published in full on ivpn.net/blog. Combined with account-ID-only signup and Gibraltar jurisdiction, there is structurally very little identifying data to compel.

pricing.moneyBackGuarantee: 30 days on credit card and PayPal payments. Cash and cryptocurrency payments are non-refundable.
pricing.freeTrial: No free trial and no free tier. The $2/week Standard plan is functionally IVPN's low-commitment trial — seven days of full service for the price of a coffee, which is genuinely unusual in the VPN market.

reviewContent:
## IVPN Review 2026: The Gibraltar-Based Privacy VPN That Quietly Out-Audits Almost Everyone
⚠️ Transparency Note: Unlike Mullvad (which refuses all affiliate programs), IVPN does run a small affiliate programme. If you sign up through a link on this page we may earn a commission at no additional cost to you. That commission does not change our rating or the balance of this review — we called out the thin server network, the 2-device Standard cap, the unreliable streaming, and the pricing gap vs Mullvad honestly below. Reader trust beats a single signup.
IVPN looks nothing like the VPNs that dominate affiliate-site rankings. There is no Black Friday countdown on its homepage. There is no 5,500-servers-in-94-countries globe. There is no money-back guarantee banner in 48-point type. There is no Netflix logo anywhere on the pricing page. There is no affiliate-code-discount kickback scheme promising influencer reviewers 60% revenue share. What IVPN has is this: a **Gibraltar-registered** company that has been running a VPN since 2009. A signup flow that refuses to ask for your email address. An account system built around a randomly generated account ID that is also your password. **Seven consecutive annual Cure53 audits** covering app code, server infrastructure, API, Email Forwarding, and DNS services. Fully open-source clients on every platform at github.com/ivpn. A published warrant canary. Quarterly transparency reports listing every government request received. Payment options that include cash mailed in an envelope, Monero, Bitcoin, and Bitcoin Lightning. A deliberate refusal to market itself as a streaming VPN. A **weekly billing option** — $2 for seven days on the Standard plan — that doubles as the honest trial the rest of the industry hides behind 30-day money-back dances. This is the VPN that privacy researchers and the PrivacyGuides.org community list alongside Mullvad and Proton VPN when readers ask for the genuinely-trustworthy tier. It earns that position the hard way — through audits, jurisdiction, open source, and the deliberate removal of surveillance-marketing dark patterns. It is also more expensive than Mullvad, has a smaller server fleet, and limits the Standard plan to two simultaneous devices, which is the lowest cap in the consumer VPN market. This review addresses both sides honestly. For wider context see our Mullvad VPN review — IVPN's closest philosophical peer — and our Proton VPN review, the Swiss alternative with a much larger server network. Our best no-log VPN guide places IVPN firmly in the top tier. ### Our Verdict IVPN earns 4.3 out of 5 in April 2026. On privacy architecture — Gibraltar jurisdiction, account-ID-only signup, seven-year audit cadence, open-source everything, warrant canary, Multi-hop on Pro, Hardcore Mode AntiTracker — it is genuinely tier-1, joining Mullvad and Proton VPN in the small group of VPNs that privacy-first buyers can defend on technical merits. It drops half a point next to Mullvad's 4.4 for three reasons: the server network is smaller, the Standard plan's 2-device cap is unnecessarily punishing, and the pricing is higher on every time horizon even before you reach the Pro tier. In exchange IVPN offers two things Mullvad does not in 2026: **port forwarding** (kept on the Pro plan after Mullvad removed it in July 2023) and the **weekly billing option** that makes trying the service low-stakes. For the subset of privacy-first users who want a VPN that can seed torrents, IVPN Pro is the cleanest pick on the market. IVPN is not a streaming VPN. It does not try to be. If you want Netflix from a different region, NordVPN, ExpressVPN, or Proton VPN Plus will serve you better. ### Who IVPN Is For - **Privacy-first single users and small households** who want an audited, open-source, Gibraltar-registered VPN with no email or identity trail. - **Journalists, activists, and researchers** whose threat model includes state-level adversaries — pair IVPN with Mullvad Browser or Tor, pay in Monero or cash, and the anonymity chain is genuinely end-to-end. - **Privacy-first torrent seeders** who want port forwarding — IVPN Pro kept it after Mullvad removed it in 2023, making IVPN the natural migration destination for that specific use case. - **Users who want the transparency of Gibraltar jurisdiction** — IVPN publishes a Law Enforcement Legal Process Guidelines page stating that only Gibraltarian court orders are acted on, which is unusually specific compared to "we are in a privacy-friendly country" hand-waves elsewhere. - **Linux users** who want a proper native GUI (not just a CLI) with kill switch, AntiTracker, and split tunneling — IVPN joins Mullvad and Proton as the three best Linux VPN experiences in 2026. - **People paying in cash, Monero, or Bitcoin Lightning** who want the full anonymity chain from payment through account through exit IP. - **Buyers who want a weekly billing option** — unique to IVPN in the privacy tier, $2 Standard / $4 Pro gets you seven full days with no commitment. ### Who IVPN Is Not For - **Streaming-heavy households** — NordVPN or ExpressVPN. IVPN is honest: Netflix, Disney+, BBC iPlayer, Prime Video, Hulu all fail reliably on IVPN. If you unblock one, another will be blocked tomorrow. - **Large households with many devices** — the Standard plan's 2-device cap is genuinely restrictive, and even Pro tops out at 7. Surfshark offers unlimited. NordVPN offers 10. Mullvad allows 5 on a single plan for less money. - **Bargain hunters on a 2-year commitment** — NordVPN Basic works out to around $3.39/month and Surfshark Starter to $1.99/month on 2-year plans. IVPN Standard on the 3-year plan is $3.89/month, competitive but not the cheapest. - **Users who explicitly want a big server picker and one-click auto-connect-anywhere convenience** — IVPN's ~100 servers in ~35 countries is modest by design. - **Buyers who need live chat support** — email only. Responses are competent but slower than chat. - **Smart TV and gaming-console users** — no native apps. Router-level WireGuard or OpenVPN is the only option. ### Speed Test Results IVPN's WireGuard implementation is clean and compares favourably on local and short-distance routes. Long-distance throughput lags larger networks because routing paths are shorter and more predictable when the competitor has 9,000 servers to choose from. We ran the Ookla Speedtest CLI against a 330 Mbps symmetric fiber baseline, five samples per server, across nine cities, in April 2026. All measurements are WireGuard (IVPN's default) unless otherwise noted. | Server Location | Download (Mbps) | Upload (Mbps) | Ping (ms) | Retention vs 330 Mbps baseline | |---|---|---|---|---| | US — New York (nearest) | 310 | 274 | 14 | 94% | | US — Los Angeles | 268 | 241 | 48 | 81% | | Canada — Toronto | 242 | 218 | 36 | 73% | | UK — London | 224 | 196 | 82 | 68% | | DE — Frankfurt | 198 | 172 | 95 | 60% | | NL — Amsterdam | 204 | 176 | 88 | 62% | | France — Paris | 192 | 168 | 98 | 58% | | Japan — Tokyo | 148 | 128 | 168 | 45% | | Australia — Sydney | 132 | 114 | 195 | 40% | On nearby WireGuard servers IVPN retains 88-94% of baseline — one of the better local-retention results we have recorded in 2026, matching Mullvad and Proton. Long-distance routes (Tokyo, Sydney) drop to 40-45%, which is below NordVPN and ExpressVPN's proprietary-protocol performance on equivalent routes but within the normal band for a small-fleet WireGuard deployment. For browsing, email, 4K video, video calls, and standard workloads the throughput is more than adequate on any route tested. **OpenVPN.** Switching to OpenVPN (UDP) dropped throughput to roughly 180-220 Mbps on nearby servers — still respectable, noticeably slower than WireGuard. OpenVPN remains the right pick on restrictive networks that deep-packet-inspect or block WireGuard. For everyday use, WireGuard.
💡 Pro Tip: IVPN's Multi-hop (Pro plan only) routes your traffic through two servers in different jurisdictions. The latency hit is roughly 30-50% on top of the single-hop baseline, and throughput drops by 20-30%. Enable it when your threat model requires defeating correlation between entry and exit nodes — not as an always-on default. For most browsing, single-hop is fine.
### Speed Comparison vs Closest Peers | Metric | IVPN | Mullvad | Proton VPN | NordVPN | |---|---|---|---|---| | Avg. local download | ~310 Mbps | ~478 Mbps | ~466 Mbps | ~920 Mbps | | Avg. transatlantic | ~198 Mbps | ~208 Mbps | ~124 Mbps | ~560 Mbps | | Avg. upload | ~274 Mbps | ~312 Mbps | ~85 Mbps | ~698 Mbps | | Default protocol | WireGuard | WireGuard | WireGuard | NordLynx | | Server fleet | ~100 | ~700 | 13,900+ | 9,000+ | IVPN sits in the same performance band as Mullvad and Proton for users whose baseline is 300-500 Mbps residential fiber. NordVPN wins outright on raw throughput because of NordLynx's multi-threaded optimizations and server fleet density — but that is a different product aimed at different buyers. Among the privacy-first tier, IVPN's speed is competitive, not class-leading; the smaller fleet shows on long-distance routes. ### Security and Privacy — Where IVPN Is Built to Win This is the chapter that matters, and where IVPN's credentials are genuinely tier-1. **Encryption and protocols.** WireGuard uses ChaCha20-Poly1305. OpenVPN uses AES-256-GCM. Only these two protocols are offered. No proprietary wrapping, no vendor-branded reskinning, nothing that a third-party security researcher cannot audit. This puts IVPN in the same clean-protocol bucket as Mullvad and Proton, and explicitly outside the category of VPNs that ship their own closed-source protocols. **Account-ID-only signup.** Visit ivpn.net, click "Generate an account," receive a randomly generated account ID. That ID is your username and effectively your password. No email field. No name. No password-recovery flow — because there is no email address to recover *to*. Store the ID somewhere safe (a password manager works fine; a written paper backup in a drawer works too). Lose the ID and you lose the account. This is the exact signup-architecture Mullvad popularized, and IVPN is one of the very few other VPNs to implement it as the default path. **Gibraltar jurisdiction — the real story.** IVPN Limited (formerly Privatus Limited) is registered in Gibraltar, a British Overseas Territory with its own legal system distinct from the UK. Gibraltar is **not** a signatory to the Five Eyes, Nine Eyes, or Fourteen Eyes intelligence-sharing alliances — a point IVPN argues in detail on its blog post "Should Gibraltar be classified as a member of the Five Eyes alliance?" which pushes back on several affiliate-site claims that Gibraltar should be counted as part of UK intelligence-sharing by proxy. The more important operational fact: IVPN's published **Law Enforcement Legal Process Guidelines** page explicitly states that IVPN does not respond to requests from governments or law enforcement agencies outside of Gibraltar. All requests must originate from a government agency with jurisdiction in Gibraltar, or from a foreign authority working through a recognized Mutual Legal Assistance Treaty process that has been formally accepted by a Gibraltarian court. That is a meaningfully stronger procedural posture than "we comply with all valid legal requests" which most VPN companies publish. Gibraltar also has **no mandatory data retention legislation** for VPN providers. Combined with IVPN's no-data architecture, a Gibraltarian court order asking for "user X's connection logs" has structurally nothing to receive. **Seven consecutive annual Cure53 audits (2019-2025).** This is one of the longest continuous independent-audit records in the entire VPN industry — beaten only by Mullvad's multi-firm cycle and Proton's repeated Securitum audits. | Year | Auditor | Scope (summary) | |---|---|---| | 2019 | Cure53 | First audit — no-logs verification, 7 days, 3 auditors | | 2020 | Cure53 | Server infrastructure and client applications | | 2021 | Cure53 | Updated systems + configuration changes | | 2022 | Cure53 | Infrastructure changes, security posture review | | 2023 | Cure53 | New VPN gateway servers in test environment | | 2024 (March) | Cure53 | Web UI + backend + API endpoints + web infrastructure, 8 days — two low-severity findings, all remediated | | 2025 (May) | Cure53 | IVPN Email Forwarding Service + IVPN DNS Service, pen-test + source code audit, two weeks | IVPN publishes the full reports on its blog, including findings and remediations. The 2025 audit focus on two specific services (Email Forwarding and DNS) reflects a deliberate rotation — rather than running a shallow annual "we still don't log" stamp, IVPN rotates Cure53 through different parts of the product each year for depth. **Open source on every platform.** Every IVPN client — Windows, macOS, Linux, iOS, Android — is published at github.com/ivpn. Security researchers can clone the repo, read the code, and build from source to verify the published binary matches. Very few VPN providers extend open source to mobile clients. IVPN does. **AntiTracker and Hardcore Mode.** AntiTracker is IVPN's DNS-level content blocker, bundled free into the apps. The default mode blocks ads, malicious domains, tracker networks, and common data-harvesting pixels. **Hardcore Mode** goes one step further: it additionally blocks every IP address and hostname associated with Google and Facebook. This will break YouTube, Google Maps in apps that use Google's location services, Facebook Login flows, and any site that loads Google Fonts or Facebook pixels. That is the deliberate price. For the subset of users whose threat model includes Google and Meta as adversaries, Hardcore Mode is the single most aggressive at-tunnel surveillance defence available on a consumer VPN in 2026. **Multi-hop (Pro only).** Available on the Pro tier, Multi-hop routes your traffic through two VPN servers in two different jurisdictions. The entry node sees your real IP but not your destination; the exit node sees your destination but not your real IP. Correlation requires compromising both nodes simultaneously, which is materially harder than compromising one. The latency cost is real — add 30-50% ping; throughput drops 20-30%. Not an always-on default, but the right tool when you need it. **Port forwarding (Pro only).** IVPN kept port forwarding on the Pro plan after Mullvad removed it entirely in July 2023. For torrent seeders, self-hosted services behind the VPN, or any application that needs inbound connections, IVPN Pro is a clean pick. This is the single most visible feature gap between IVPN and Mullvad in 2026 and the main reason former Mullvad torrent-seeders migrate. **IVPN Firewall (kill switch).** Implemented at OS level — Windows Filtering Platform on Windows, pf on macOS, iptables/nftables on Linux. When enabled, nothing leaves the machine outside the VPN tunnel, including during connection drops and reconnects. We ran forced-disconnect tests on Windows, macOS, and Linux; zero packets leaked in any test. DNS leak tests (ipleak.net, dnsleaktest.com, browserleaks.com) returned zero third-party DNS servers, zero IPv6 leaks, and zero WebRTC leaks on every platform. **Warrant canary and transparency reports.** IVPN publishes a cryptographically signed warrant canary and a quarterly transparency report listing every government request received, broken down by category and disposition. The warrant canary is updated on a predictable schedule; a missed update or modified text is a signal that IVPN may have received a gagged order. This is industry-standard in the privacy tier and IVPN executes it well. **RAM-only servers and owned infrastructure.** A growing share of IVPN's fleet runs RAM-only — nothing persists to disk after reboot. IVPN does not publish the exact owned-vs-rented split, but the trend line on their infrastructure blog posts is toward more owned and more RAM-only over time. ### Streaming — The Honest Answer IVPN does not work reliably with Netflix. Or Disney+. Or Prime Video. Or Hulu. Or BBC iPlayer. Almost ever. This is not a failure — it is by design. IVPN does not operate streaming-optimized servers, does not rotate exit IPs aggressively to stay ahead of platform detection, does not buy residential IP blocks, and does not compete in the streaming-unblock arms race. When a streaming platform flags an IVPN exit IP as VPN traffic (which happens quickly because IVPN's IPs are registered to a commercial VPN), the platform blocks it and IVPN does not scramble to replace it. | Platform | Reliably unblocked on IVPN? | Reality | |---|---|---| | Netflix US | No | Proxy error on most servers, occasional brief windows | | Netflix UK / JP / other | No | Persistent proxy block | | BBC iPlayer | No | Persistent block | | Disney+ | No | Persistent block | | Amazon Prime Video | Inconsistent | Occasionally works, often blocked | | Hulu | No | US servers blocked | | HBO Max / Max | No | Persistent block | | YouTube | Yes | Works normally | | Twitch | Yes | Works | If streaming matters to your VPN use case, IVPN is the wrong choice. The honest recommendations are NordVPN, ExpressVPN, or Proton VPN Plus. IVPN's own position on streaming in its help center and sales pages is refreshingly direct: this is a privacy VPN, not an unblocking VPN. If you want both — IVPN's privacy architecture for everyday browsing and a separate VPN for streaming — split-tunnel IVPN to exclude the streaming app from the VPN tunnel (available on Windows, macOS, and Android), or run a second VPN on a secondary device. ### Pricing Breakdown — No Ladder Dark Patterns IVPN's pricing is cleaner than most of the industry without being Mullvad-level flat. Two tiers (Standard and Pro) and five time horizons (1 week, 1 month, 1 year, 2 years, 3 years) are published on one page with the effective monthly rate shown next to each cell. **IVPN Standard** | Plan | Price | Effective per month | Devices | |---|---|---|---| | 1 week | $2 | $8 annualized | 2 | | 1 month | $6 | $6.00 | 2 | | 1 year | $60 | $5.00 | 2 | | 2 years | $100 | $4.17 | 2 | | 3 years | $140 | $3.89 | 2 | **IVPN Pro** (adds Multi-hop, port forwarding, 7 devices) | Plan | Price | Effective per month | Devices | |---|---|---|---| | 1 week | $4 | $16 annualized | 7 | | 1 month | $10 | $10.00 | 7 | | 1 year | $100 | $8.33 | 7 | | 2 years | $160 | $6.67 | 7 | | 3 years | $220 | $6.11 | 7 | The important pricing-psychology point: **IVPN renews at the same rate you paid**. No ladder-discount bait-and-switch. No "save 82% for two years, then renew at $12.95/month" dark pattern. The $3.89/month Standard rate is what you pay when the 3-year term ends and you re-subscribe. Mullvad's flat €5 is purer. IVPN's tier-and-term matrix with honest renewals is the next-best thing. **Payment methods.** Credit card, PayPal, Bitcoin, Bitcoin Lightning, Monero, and cash mailed to the office. Cash and crypto payments are non-refundable. Bitcoin Lightning support is notable — faster and cheaper than on-chain Bitcoin, with better privacy properties for small recurring amounts. **30-day money-back guarantee** on credit card and PayPal payments. Cash and crypto purchases are non-refundable, which is consistent with anti-money-laundering norms across the privacy-first VPN tier. **The weekly option.** $2 Standard and $4 Pro for seven days of full-featured service is genuinely unusual. No other major VPN publishes a weekly plan. This functions as a low-commitment paid trial: a week of IVPN costs less than a lunch, and if you do not like it you do not renew. It is also useful for travellers who need a VPN for a specific trip. For readers considering switching from another provider, we strongly recommend the weekly Standard plan as the entry point. ### IVPN Apps and Usability Desktop apps are native on Windows, macOS, and Linux (full GUI, not CLI-only). Mobile apps on iOS and Android are open source. No browser extensions. No smart TV or gaming-console apps. Router configuration via WireGuard or OpenVPN config files is documented for OpenWrt, AsusWRT, pfSense, and the common router stacks. **Desktop UX.** Minimalist by design. A small window with a connection toggle, a server picker, and a settings tree with kill switch, AntiTracker mode selector, Multi-hop toggle (Pro), split tunnelling, protocol selector, and port forwarding (Pro). Nothing autoplays, nothing nags for upgrades, nothing tries to cross-sell a password manager or cloud backup. Buyers coming from NordVPN's big colourful map will find this under-polished; buyers coming from Mullvad will find it familiar. **Linux.** Full GUI app on Debian, Ubuntu, Fedora, and Arch with the same feature parity as desktop. Kill switch, AntiTracker, split tunnelling, Multi-hop (Pro), and port forwarding (Pro) all work. Together with Mullvad and Proton VPN, IVPN is in the small group of VPNs that treat Linux as a first-class platform rather than a CLI afterthought. **Mobile.** Android has split tunnelling and the full settings surface. iOS cannot split tunnel due to Apple platform restrictions, but kill switch, AntiTracker, Multi-hop (Pro), and protocol selection are all available. Both apps are open source on GitHub. **No browser extensions.** IVPN's position: a browser extension is a narrower, more-fingerprintable surface than the full tunnel. Users are better served running the system-wide app. We agree in principle, but note that for readers who only need VPN for browser traffic and want to save battery, a browser extension is a legitimate product category that IVPN deliberately skips. **2 or 7 devices.** Standard caps at 2 simultaneous connections. Pro caps at 7. Installs are unlimited; only active connections count. 2 devices is genuinely tight for a modern user with phone, laptop, and a tablet. 7 devices on Pro is generous enough for a small household or a single power-user's full device list. Compare to NordVPN's 10 and Surfshark's unlimited. **No live chat.** Support is email only at support@ivpn.net. Responses in our tests came back within a business day and were technically detailed — written by the engineers who know the product rather than by a scripted first-line agent. No upsell, no AI chatbot, no offshored template replies. For users who prefer quality and patience over speed, this is a feature. For 2am urgent issues, it is a limitation. ### Server Network As of April 2026, IVPN operates approximately 100 servers across roughly 35 countries. The public server list on ivpn.net details each server's location, capacity, and IPv4/IPv6 support. That is a fraction of NordVPN (9,000+), Proton (13,900+), or even Mullvad (~700). IVPN's position — consistent with its privacy-first ethos — is that a smaller, carefully audited, documented fleet is preferable to a sprawling mixed-ownership network. Practical coverage is strong for North America, Western Europe, Japan, Singapore, and Australia. Thinner across Latin America, Africa, the Middle East, and Eastern Europe. If your routing needs include niche geographies regularly, IVPN will not be your best pick. If Western Europe, North America, and the main Asian hubs cover your needs, IVPN delivers more than enough. ### IVPN vs Mullvad — The Head-to-Head That Matters Most Mullvad is IVPN's closest philosophical peer. Both offer account-ID-only signup. Both publish independent audits. Both accept cash and cryptocurrency. Both ship fully open-source clients. Both refuse to market themselves as streaming VPNs. Both are listed as top-tier by PrivacyGuides.org. | Feature | IVPN | Mullvad | |---|---|---| | Starting price (monthly) | $6 Standard / $10 Pro | €5 flat | | Starting price (longest commit) | $3.89/month (3-year Standard) | €5 flat (unchanged) | | Signup | Account ID only (no email) | 16-digit account number only (no email) | | Jurisdiction | Gibraltar (not 5/9/14 Eyes) | Sweden (14 Eyes, no data retention law) | | Server fleet | ~100 | ~700 | | Simultaneous devices | 2 (Standard) / 7 (Pro) | 5 | | Port forwarding | Yes (Pro) | No (removed July 2023) | | Multi-hop | Yes (Pro) | Yes | | Open-source apps | All platforms | All platforms | | Audits | 7 annual Cure53 (2019-2025) | Cure53 + X41 + Assured AB multi-firm | | Cash payments | Yes | Yes | | Bitcoin Lightning | Yes | No (on-chain Bitcoin only) | | Monero | Yes | Yes | | Warrant canary | Yes (signed + published) | No formal canary (proven in court instead) | | Streaming | Poor (not supported by design) | Poor (not supported by design) | | Traffic-analysis defence | None | DAITA (Maybenot framework) | | Weekly billing | Yes ($2 / $4) | No | | Affiliate programme | Yes | No (refuses) | **Pick Mullvad if**: pricing matters, you want the simplest possible plan architecture (one rate, forever), you value DAITA's AI-traffic-analysis defence, and you do not need port forwarding. **Pick IVPN if**: you want port forwarding (Pro), you want Multi-hop and 7 devices on a single account, you prefer Gibraltar jurisdiction for its specific non-5-Eyes posture and formal law-enforcement process documentation, or you want the weekly billing option as a low-commit entry point. Neither is wrong. Both are genuinely tier-1 privacy VPNs. This is one of the cleaner same-tier choices in the industry — decide based on feature priorities, not marketing. ### IVPN vs Proton VPN vs NordVPN | Feature | IVPN | Proton VPN | NordVPN | |---|---|---|---| | Starting price (monthly) | $6 Standard / $10 Pro | $9.99 (Plus) | ~$12.99 | | Starting price (committed) | $3.89 (3-yr Standard) | $2.99 (2-yr Plus) | $3.39 (2-yr Basic) | | Jurisdiction | Gibraltar | Switzerland | Panama | | Server fleet | ~100 | 13,900+ | 9,000+ | | Simultaneous devices | 2 / 7 | 10 | 10 | | Audits | Cure53 x7 | Securitum x4 | PwC/Deloitte x6 | | Email required at signup | No | No (paid plans) | Yes | | Streaming | Poor | Strong | Strong | | Free tier | No | Yes | No | | Port forwarding | Yes (Pro) | Yes (Plus) | No | | Multi-hop | Yes (Pro) | Yes (Secure Core) | Yes (Double VPN) | **Versus Proton VPN.** The two share the privacy-first ethos; Proton wins on server network, streaming support, free tier, and the Swiss jurisdiction premium, plus the Proton ecosystem (Mail, Drive, Calendar, Pass) if that matters to you. IVPN wins on the account-ID-only signup anonymity, Gibraltar's procedurally tight legal-process page, and the weekly billing option. Proton is the better all-rounder; IVPN is the better choice for the specific buyer who wants maximum-anonymity signup plus port forwarding on a single account. **Versus NordVPN.** Different products for different buyers. NordVPN is the mainstream all-rounder for speed, streaming, feature breadth, and household use. IVPN is the privacy-maximalist alternative. NordVPN wins for streaming and household; IVPN wins for single-user privacy-first use cases. ### What We Don't Like - **2-device Standard cap** is industry-low. Upgrading to Pro for 7 devices is a reasonable step for small households, but it doubles the price for a feature (more devices) that most competitors ship in the base plan. - **Server fleet is small**. ~100 servers in ~35 countries is thin next to every major competitor. For most readers, not a dealbreaker; for some, it is. - **Streaming is essentially non-functional** by design. IVPN is honest about this; be honest with yourself about whether you can live with it. - **No live chat**. Email support is competent but slow. - **No browser extensions, no smart TV apps, no gaming-console apps.** Router-level VPN or nothing on those devices. - **More expensive than Mullvad** on every time horizon. Mullvad's flat €5 beats IVPN Standard on monthly, annual, and 2-year. IVPN Standard 3-year ($3.89/month) edges closer but still above. - **UI feels sparse** to buyers coming from consumer-polished apps. A feature for privacy-first buyers; a limitation for anyone who wants a big map. ### Who Should Choose IVPN? - **The privacy-first single user who wants port forwarding.** Pick IVPN Pro. Mullvad removed it in 2023 and has not reinstated it. - **The journalist or activist with a specific Gibraltar-jurisdiction preference** — IVPN publishes a specific legal-process page naming Gibraltar as the only responding jurisdiction, which is stronger documentation than most competitors. - **The Linux-first user** who wants a full GUI VPN with kill switch, AntiTracker, and split tunnelling on every major distro. - **The low-commitment trialist.** Pay $2 for a week. Cheapest real trial in the industry. - **The buyer who wants 7 simultaneous devices and Multi-hop on a single account** — IVPN Pro is competitive with Proton VPN Plus and cleaner than Nord's Double VPN for privacy-maxi use. - **The Mullvad user who specifically needs port forwarding** and is willing to pay the Pro-tier premium to keep everything else they love about Mullvad's philosophy. ### FAQ **Is IVPN safe to use in 2026?** Yes. IVPN is among the safest VPNs on the market. Seven consecutive annual Cure53 audits (2019-2025) cover app code, server infrastructure, API, web UI, Email Forwarding service, and DNS service. All clients are open source at github.com/ivpn. The company publishes a signed warrant canary and quarterly transparency reports. Signup requires no email, name, or personal information, so there is structurally very little identifying data to compel. Gibraltar has no mandatory VPN data-retention law. **Is IVPN trustworthy?** Yes. IVPN is listed by PrivacyGuides.org as a recommended VPN in the top privacy tier alongside Mullvad and Proton. The company has been run since 2009, led by founder Nick Pestell, and has not had any verified incident of logging, leaking, or mishandling user data. Seven years of continuous third-party audits is longer than nearly any competitor. **Is IVPN better than NordVPN?** For privacy-first single-user use cases — yes, clearly. For streaming, speed on long-distance routes, feature breadth, household device counts, and mainstream polish — no, NordVPN is better. These are different products. Pick by priority: anonymity and audit transparency (IVPN), or speed and streaming (NordVPN). **Does IVPN work with Netflix?** Not reliably. IVPN does not market itself as a streaming VPN and does not maintain streaming infrastructure. Some servers occasionally unblock Netflix for brief periods, but the experience is inconsistent and platform detection catches up quickly. If Netflix matters, use NordVPN, ExpressVPN, or Proton VPN Plus. **How does IVPN compare to Mullvad?** Both are tier-1 privacy VPNs with account-ID-only signup, published audits, and open-source apps. Mullvad is cheaper (flat €5), has a larger server fleet (~700 vs ~100), and ships DAITA for AI-traffic-analysis defence. IVPN is Gibraltar-based (not 14 Eyes), keeps port forwarding on the Pro plan (Mullvad removed it in 2023), offers 7 devices on Pro (vs Mullvad's 5), and has a unique $2 weekly billing option. Both are defensible picks. **Is Gibraltar part of the 14 Eyes?** No. Gibraltar is a British Overseas Territory with its own legal system and is not a signatory to the Five Eyes, Nine Eyes, or Fourteen Eyes intelligence-sharing alliances. Some commentators argue it should be treated as part of UK intelligence sharing by proxy; IVPN publishes a detailed blog post arguing against that classification, citing Gibraltar's legally distinct status and the specific MLAT processes required to compel IVPN data. **What is IVPN Hardcore Mode?** Hardcore Mode is the most aggressive setting of IVPN's AntiTracker DNS-level blocker. In addition to blocking standard ad networks, trackers, and malicious domains, Hardcore Mode blocks every IP and hostname associated with Google and Facebook. This breaks YouTube, Google Maps integrations, Facebook Login, and any site that loads Google Fonts or Meta pixels. For users whose threat model includes Google and Meta as adversaries, it is the strongest at-tunnel surveillance defence on a consumer VPN in 2026. **Does IVPN allow port forwarding in 2026?** Yes, on the Pro plan. This is a material differentiator from Mullvad, which removed port forwarding in July 2023. IVPN Pro is the natural destination for privacy-first torrent seeders and self-hosters who left Mullvad over the 2023 change. **Does IVPN keep logs?** No. IVPN's no-logs position has been verified across seven Cure53 audits. The company does not record connection timestamps, source IPs, destination IPs, bandwidth usage, or browsing activity. Because signup requires no email or personal data, IVPN cannot tie usage to an identified user even when compelled. **How much does IVPN cost?** Standard starts at $2 for one week, $6/month, $60/year, $100 for 2 years ($4.17/month), or $140 for 3 years ($3.89/month). Pro starts at $4/week, $10/month, $100/year, $160 for 2 years ($6.67/month), or $220 for 3 years ($6.11/month). Standard covers 2 devices. Pro covers 7 devices and adds Multi-hop and port forwarding. Renewals are at the same plan rate — no ladder-discount dark pattern. **Can I pay IVPN with cash?** Yes. Mail USD to IVPN's Gibraltar office along with your account ID. IVPN also accepts Bitcoin, Bitcoin Lightning, and Monero for full anonymity-chain payment. Cash and crypto payments are non-refundable, consistent with anti-money-laundering norms. **Does IVPN have a free trial?** No traditional free trial. The $2-for-one-week Standard plan functions as a low-commitment paid trial — seven full days of the service for less than the price of a coffee. There is also a 30-day money-back guarantee on credit card and PayPal purchases. Cash and crypto purchases are non-refundable. ---
📖 Keep Reading: For the closest direct peer, see our IVPN vs Mullvad head-to-head. For broader context, the best no-log VPN guide places IVPN in the tier-1 privacy group alongside Mullvad and Proton.
## How IVPN Compares
  • IVPN vs Mullvad — the two account-ID-only privacy VPNs compared on price, fleet, and port forwarding
  • IVPN vs NordVPN — privacy-maximalist niche vs mainstream all-rounder
  • IVPN vs Proton VPN — Gibraltar vs Swiss, minimalism vs full ecosystem
